title: Two-Weapon Fighting
summary: Rules for fighting with a weapon in each hand.
content:
## Two-Weapon Fighting

When you take the Attack action and attack with a light melee weapon that you're holding in one hand, you can use a bonus action to attack with a different light melee weapon that you're holding in the other hand. You don't add your ability modifier to the damage of the bonus attack, unless that modifier is negative.

If either weapon has the thrown property, you can throw the weapon, instead of making a melee attack with it.

title: Mounted Combat
summary: Rules for fighting while riding a mount, including controlled and independent mounts.
content:
## Mounted Combat

A willing creature that is at least one size larger than you and that has an appropriate anatomy can serve as a mount.

### Mounting and Dismounting
Once during your move, you can mount or dismount a creature that is within 5 feet of you. Doing so costs an amount of movement equal to **half your speed**. If an effect moves your mount against its will while you're on it, you must succeed on a **DC 10 Dexterity saving throw** or fall off, landing prone within 5 feet. If you're knocked prone while mounted, you must make the same saving throw. If your mount is knocked prone, you can use your **reaction** to dismount as it falls and land on your feet. Otherwise, you are dismounted and fall prone within 5 feet.

### Controlling a Mount
- **Controlled mount** (intelligent mounts may act independently): The mount acts on your initiative. It can move and use the Dash, Disengage, or Dodge action. It cannot attack on the turn you mount it.
- **Independent mount**: Retains its own initiative and full range of actions. It moves and acts as it wishes.

### Attacks While Mounted
You can attack normally while mounted. If the mount provokes an opportunity attack, the attacker can target you or the mount.

title: Underwater Combat
summary: Rules for fighting underwater, including weapon restrictions and swimming penalties.
content:
## Underwater Combat

When making a **melee weapon attack**, a creature that doesn't have a swimming speed (either natural or granted by magic) has **disadvantage** on the attack roll unless the weapon is a dagger, javelin, shortsword, spear, or trident.

### Ranged Attacks
A **ranged weapon attack** automatically **misses** a target beyond the weapon's normal range. Even against a target within normal range, the attack roll has **disadvantage** unless the weapon is a crossbow, a net, or a weapon that is thrown like a javelin (including a spear, trident, or dart).

### Creatures and Water
Creatures fully immersed in water have **resistance to fire damage**. A creature with a natural swimming speed can fight underwater without penalty.

title: Concentration
summary: Rules for maintaining concentration on spells during combat.
content:
## Concentration

Some spells require you to maintain **concentration** in order to keep their magic active. If you lose concentration, such a spell ends.

### Maintaining Concentration
You can maintain concentration on a spell for up to the spell's listed duration. You can end concentration at any time (no action required).

### Breaking Concentration
Concentration can be broken by:

1. **Casting another concentration spell.** You lose concentration on the current spell immediately when you begin casting a new concentration spell.
2. **Taking damage.** Make a **Constitution saving throw** whenever you take damage while concentrating. The DC equals **10** or **half the damage taken**, whichever is higher. If you take damage from multiple sources (e.g., multiple missiles), you make a separate saving throw for each source of damage.
3. **Being incapacitated or killed.** Concentration ends immediately.
4. **Environmental interference.** The DM might decide that certain environmental phenomena (a wave crashing over you on a storm-tossed ship) require a DC 10 Constitution saving throw to maintain concentration.

### One Spell at a Time
You can only concentrate on **one spell at a time**. Non-concentration spells can be active simultaneously without conflict.

title: Temporary Hit Points
summary: Rules for temporary hit points, including stacking and duration.
content:
## Temporary Hit Points

Some spells and special abilities confer **temporary hit points** to a creature. Temporary hit points aren't actual hit points; they are a buffer against damage, a pool of hit points that protect you from injury.

### Key Rules
- **Damage applies to temporary hit points first.** Any leftover damage carries over to your normal hit points.
- **Temporary hit points don't stack.** If you have temporary hit points and receive more of them, you choose whether to keep the ones you have or gain the new ones. You cannot add them together. For example, if a spell grants you 12 temporary hit points when you already have 10, you can have 12 or 10, not 22.
- **They can't be healed.** Because temporary hit points are separate from your actual hit points, they can't be restored by any healing. They are lost when used to absorb damage.
- **Duration.** Temporary hit points last until they're depleted or you finish a **long rest**, unless a feature that grants them specifies a different duration.
- **Not real hit points.** Temporary hit points don't count as actual hit points for any purpose (they can't take you above your hit point maximum). A creature at 0 hit points that receives temporary hit points is still at 0 hit points but has the temporary buffer.

title: Death Saving Throws
summary: Rules for death saving throws at 0 HP: DC 10, 3 successes stabilize, 3 failures mean death, natural 20 restores 1 HP.
content:
## Death Saving Throws

Whenever you start your turn with **0 hit points**, you must make a special saving throw, called a **death saving throw**, to determine whether you creep closer to death or hang onto life.

### Making Death Saving Throws
- **DC 10.** Roll a d20. A result of **10 or higher** is a success; below 10 is a failure. Ability scores and modifiers don't apply (unless a feature says otherwise).
- **Three successes** = you become **stable** (see Stabilizing a Creature).
- **Three failures** = you **die**.
- Successes and failures don't need to be consecutive; keep track of both until you collect three of a kind.

### Rolling a 1 or 20
- **Natural 1**: Counts as **two failures**.
- **Natural 20**: You regain **1 hit point** and become conscious. All death save successes and failures reset.

### Damage at 0 Hit Points
- If you take damage while at 0 hit points, you suffer a **death saving throw failure**.
- If the damage is from a **critical hit**, it counts as **two failures** instead.
- If the damage equals or exceeds your **hit point maximum**, you suffer **instant death**.

### Resetting
Death saving throw successes and failures reset to zero whenever you regain any hit points or become stable.

title: Stabilizing a Creature
summary: Rules for stabilizing a dying creature using a Medicine check (DC 10) or spare the dying.
content:
## Stabilizing a Creature

You can use your **action** to administer first aid to an unconscious creature and attempt to stabilize it.

### Medicine Check
Make a **Wisdom (Medicine) check** with a **DC of 10**. On a success, the creature is **stable** -- it no longer needs to make death saving throws but remains unconscious at 0 hit points. Becoming stable doesn't restore any hit points.

### Stable Creatures
- A stable creature that isn't healed regains **1 hit point** after **1d4 hours**.
- If a stable creature takes damage, it is no longer stable and must begin making death saving throws again.

### Spare the Dying
The cantrip **spare the dying** automatically stabilizes a creature without requiring a check. It requires an action and touch range.

### Healing
Any amount of healing applied to a creature at 0 hit points makes the creature conscious. For example, a *cure wounds* spell for 1 HP wakes the creature up immediately.

title: Knocking a Creature Out
summary: Rules for choosing to knock a creature unconscious instead of killing it with a melee attack.
content:
## Knocking a Creature Out

Sometimes an attacker wants to incapacitate a foe, rather than deal a killing blow.

### Nonlethal Damage
When an attacker reduces a creature to 0 hit points with a **melee attack**, the attacker can choose to knock the creature out. The attacker makes this choice the instant the damage is dealt.

### Key Rules
- **Melee only.** Only melee attacks can knock a creature out. Ranged attacks and spells that reduce a creature to 0 hit points kill it as normal (unless the spell description says otherwise).
- **Unconscious, not dead.** The creature falls **unconscious** and is **stable** -- it doesn't need to make death saving throws.
- **Recovery.** The creature regains 1 hit point after 1d4 hours unless healed sooner.

### When to Use
This rule is useful when the party wants to capture enemies for interrogation, fulfill a bounty that requires a live target, or act mercifully.

title: Critical Hits
summary: Rules for critical hits: natural 20 auto-hits and doubles all damage dice.
content:
## Critical Hits

When you roll a **natural 20** on an attack roll, you score a critical hit, regardless of any modifiers or the target's AC.

### Doubled Dice
A critical hit means you roll **all of the attack's damage dice twice** and add them together, then add any relevant modifiers as normal.

**Example:** If you score a critical hit with a dagger, roll **2d4** for the damage rather than 1d4, then add your ability modifier. If the attack involves extra damage dice (such as from Sneak Attack or a spell like *hex*), you roll those dice twice as well.

### Natural 20 = Auto-Hit
A natural 20 on an attack roll **always hits**, even if the total would not meet or exceed the target's AC.

### Natural 1 = Auto-Miss
A natural 1 on an attack roll **always misses**, regardless of modifiers. Natural 1s and 20s only apply to **attack rolls**, not ability checks or saving throws (unless a specific rule says otherwise).

### Expanded Crit Range
Some class features (e.g., Champion Fighter's Improved Critical) expand the critical hit range to 19-20 or even 18-20. The doubled dice rule still applies.

title: Surprise
summary: Rules for determining surprise at the start of combat and how it affects a creature's first turn.
content:
## Surprise

A band of adventurers sneaks up on a bandit camp, springing from the trees to attack them. A gelatinous cube glides down a dungeon passage, unnoticed by the adventurers until it engulfs one of them. In these situations, one side of the battle gains **surprise** over the other.

### Determining Surprise
The DM determines who might be surprised. Compare the **Dexterity (Stealth)** checks of the hiding creatures against the **passive Wisdom (Perception)** score of each creature on the opposing side. Any character or monster that doesn't notice a threat is **surprised** at the start of the encounter.

### Effects of Surprise
- A surprised creature **cannot move or take actions** on its first turn of combat.
- A surprised creature **cannot take reactions** until its first turn ends.
- A member of a group can be surprised even if the other members aren't.

### Initiative Still Applies
Everyone rolls initiative as normal. A surprised creature still has a place in the initiative order -- it simply can't do anything on its first turn. After that turn passes, the creature is no longer surprised and acts normally.

### Alert Feat
The **Alert** feat prevents a character from being surprised while conscious.

title: Reactions
summary: Rules for reactions in combat: one per round, triggered by specific events.
content:
## Reactions

Certain special abilities, spells, and situations allow you to take a special action called a **reaction**. A reaction is an instant response to a trigger of some kind, which can occur on your turn or on someone else's.

### Key Rules
- **One per round.** You can take only **one reaction per round** (from the start of your turn to the start of your next turn).
- **Trigger required.** A reaction must be taken in response to a specified trigger.
- **Interrupts the trigger.** The reaction happens immediately after its trigger completes, unless the reaction's description says otherwise.
- **Refreshes on your turn.** Your reaction is available again at the **start of your turn**.

### Common Reactions
| Reaction | Trigger |
|---|---|
| **Opportunity Attack** | A hostile creature you can see moves out of your reach |
| **Shield (spell)** | You are hit by an attack or targeted by *magic missile* |
| **Counterspell** | A creature within 60 ft casts a spell |
| **Absorb Elements** | You take acid, cold, fire, lightning, or thunder damage |
| **Hellish Rebuke** | You are damaged by a creature within 60 ft |
| **Uncanny Dodge** | An attacker you can see hits you with an attack (Rogue feature) |

### Opportunity Attacks
The most common reaction. When a hostile creature that you can see **moves out of your reach**, you can use your reaction to make **one melee attack** against that creature. The attack occurs right before the creature leaves your reach. You can avoid provoking opportunity attacks by taking the **Disengage** action.

title: Bonus Actions
summary: Rules for bonus actions: one per turn, only when a feature grants one.
content:
## Bonus Actions

Various class features, spells, and other abilities let you take an additional action on your turn called a **bonus action**.

### Key Rules
- **One per turn.** You can take only **one bonus action** on your turn, so you must choose which bonus action to use if you have more than one available.
- **Only when granted.** You can only take a bonus action when a special ability, spell, or feature states that you can do something as a bonus action. Otherwise, you don't have a bonus action.
- **Timing.** You choose when to take a bonus action during your turn, unless the bonus action's timing is specified. Anything that deprives you of your ability to take actions also prevents you from taking a bonus action.

### Common Bonus Actions
| Bonus Action | Source |
|---|---|
| **Offhand attack** | Two-Weapon Fighting |
| **Cunning Action** (Dash, Disengage, Hide) | Rogue class feature |
| **Rage** | Barbarian class feature |
| **Healing Word** | Spell |
| **Misty Step** | Spell |
| **Spiritual Weapon attack** | Spell |
| **Shield Master shove** | Shield Master feat |
| **Martial Arts unarmed strike** | Monk class feature |
| **Wild Shape** | Druid class feature (some forms) |
| **Hex / Hunter's Mark move** | Spell |

title: Ready Action
summary: Rules for readying an action with a trigger, including held spells and concentration requirements.
content:
## Ready Action

Sometimes you want to get the jump on a foe or wait for a particular circumstance before you act. To do so, you can take the **Ready** action on your turn.

### How It Works
1. **Decide the trigger.** Describe a perceivable circumstance that will trigger your reaction. For example: "If the cultist steps on the trapdoor, I pull the lever."
2. **Choose your response.** When the trigger occurs, you can either take your **reaction** to respond right after the trigger finishes, or **ignore the trigger**.
3. **Use your reaction.** The readied action uses your reaction when the trigger occurs.

### Key Rules
- **Uses your action.** You spend your action on your turn to Ready.
- **Uses your reaction.** The readied response uses your reaction when triggered.
- **Lost if not triggered.** If the trigger never occurs before the start of your next turn, the readied action is lost.

### Readying a Spell
When you ready a spell, you **cast it as normal** on your turn but **hold its energy**, releasing it with your reaction when the trigger occurs.

- The spell must have a casting time of **1 action**.
- Holding a readied spell requires **concentration**. If your concentration is broken, the spell dissipates without taking effect and the spell slot is consumed.
- This means you lose concentration on any other spell you were concentrating on when you ready a spell.

### Example
A wizard readies *fireball* to cast when enemies cluster together. She casts it on her turn (expending the slot and beginning concentration), then releases it with her reaction when the trigger occurs. If she takes damage and fails the concentration check before the trigger, the spell slot is wasted.

title: Help Action
summary: Rules for the Help action: grant advantage on an ally's attack or ability check.
content:
## Help Action

You can lend your aid to another creature in the completion of a task. When you take the **Help** action, the creature you aid gains **advantage** on the next ability check it makes to perform the task you are helping with, provided it makes the check before the start of your next turn.

### Help in Combat
Alternatively, you can aid a friendly creature in attacking a creature within **5 feet of you**. You feint, distract the target, or in some other way team up to make your ally's attack more effective. If your ally attacks the target before your next turn, the first attack roll is made with **advantage**.

### Key Rules
- **Costs your action.** Taking the Help action uses your action for the turn.
- **Range.** When helping with an attack, you must be within 5 feet of the target creature.
- **One check/attack.** The advantage applies only to the **first** relevant ability check or attack roll, not to all of them.
- **Capability.** You must be able to reasonably assist. The DM may rule that Help is not possible if the character has no way to meaningfully contribute (e.g., helping pick a lock with no proficiency in thieves' tools).

title: Dodge Action
summary: Rules for the Dodge action: attacks against you have disadvantage and you gain advantage on Dexterity saving throws.
content:
## Dodge Action

When you take the **Dodge** action, you focus entirely on avoiding attacks.

### Effects
- **Attacks against you have disadvantage.** Until the start of your next turn, any attack roll made against you has **disadvantage** if you can see the attacker.
- **Dexterity saving throw advantage.** You make **Dexterity saving throws** with **advantage** until the start of your next turn.

### Key Rules
- **Costs your action.** Dodge uses your action for the turn. You can still move and take bonus actions/reactions.
- **Lost if incapacitated.** You lose the benefit of Dodge if you are **incapacitated** or if your speed drops to **0**.
- **Must see the attacker.** The disadvantage on attack rolls only applies if you can see the attacker. Invisible creatures attacking you don't suffer disadvantage from your Dodge.

### When to Use
Dodge is most effective when you expect to be targeted by many attacks, need to survive until healing arrives, or are holding a position and don't need to attack. Monks can Dodge as a bonus action using **Patient Defense** (costs 1 ki point).

title: Dash Action
summary: Rules for the Dash action: gain extra movement equal to your speed for the current turn.
content:
## Dash Action

When you take the **Dash** action, you gain extra movement for the current turn. The increase equals your **speed**, after applying any modifiers.

### Key Rules
- **Doubles effective movement.** With a speed of 30 feet, you can move up to 60 feet on your turn if you Dash.
- **Costs your action.** Dash uses your action for the turn.
- **Stacks with bonus action Dash.** If you can Dash as a bonus action (e.g., Rogue's Cunning Action or the *expeditious retreat* spell), you gain the extra movement again. A Rogue with 30 ft speed could move up to 90 feet in one turn (30 + 30 from action Dash + 30 from bonus action Dash).
- **Affected by speed modifiers.** If your speed is reduced (e.g., heavy armor without sufficient Strength, or difficult terrain), Dash gives you extra movement equal to your **modified speed**, not your base speed.
- **Difficult terrain still applies.** Difficult terrain costs double movement as normal, even during a Dash.

### When to Use
Dash is useful for closing distance with a ranged enemy, fleeing combat, or navigating a large battlefield. Rogues and Monks have class features that allow Dashing without using their action.

title: Disengage Action
summary: Rules for the Disengage action: your movement doesn't provoke opportunity attacks for the rest of the turn.
content:
## Disengage Action

If you take the **Disengage** action, your movement doesn't provoke **opportunity attacks** for the rest of the turn.

### Key Rules
- **Costs your action.** Disengage uses your action for the turn. You can still move and take bonus actions/reactions.
- **Lasts until end of turn.** The benefit lasts until the end of your current turn only, not until the start of your next turn.
- **Doesn't grant extra movement.** Disengage only prevents opportunity attacks -- it doesn't give you additional speed. Combine it with Dash (if available as a bonus action) for maximum retreat distance.

### When to Use
- Retreating from melee range of dangerous enemies.
- Repositioning a squishy character (wizard, sorcerer) without taking damage.
- Moving through a crowd of enemies to reach a priority target behind them.

### Class Features
- **Rogues** can Disengage as a bonus action via **Cunning Action**.
- **Monks** can Disengage as a bonus action via **Step of the Wind** (costs 1 ki point).
- **Goblins** have the **Nimble Escape** trait, allowing Disengage as a bonus action.

title: Hide Action
summary: Rules for the Hide action: making a Stealth check and the benefits of attacking from hidden.
content:
## Hide Action

When you take the **Hide** action, you make a **Dexterity (Stealth)** check in an attempt to hide. If you succeed, you gain certain benefits as described below.

### Requirements
- **You can't hide from a creature that can see you clearly.** You must have total cover, heavy obscurement, or be otherwise out of sight.
- **The DM sets the DC** or compares your Stealth check against the passive Perception of creatures you're hiding from.

### Benefits of Being Hidden
- **Unseen attacker advantage.** When you attack a creature that can't see you, you have **advantage** on the attack roll.
- **Location unknown.** Creatures don't know your exact location unless they detect you.

### Revealing Yourself
- **Attacking breaks hiding.** When you make an attack (hit or miss), you give away your location and are no longer hidden.
- **Making noise or being spotted.** If you step out of cover, make noise, or a creature actively searches and beats your Stealth check, you are detected.

### Key Rules
- **Costs your action** (or bonus action for Rogues via Cunning Action).
- **Passive Perception.** The DM typically compares your Stealth check to the **passive Perception** of nearby creatures.
- **Invisible creatures.** Being invisible doesn't necessarily mean hidden -- you can still be detected by noise, tracks, or other signs. But invisibility makes it much easier to hide.

### Combat Tactics
Hiding mid-combat is useful for Rogues who need advantage for **Sneak Attack**, or for any character who needs to break line of sight and reposition.

title: Flanking
summary: Optional rule for flanking: allies on opposite sides of an enemy gain advantage on melee attacks.
content:
## Flanking (Optional Rule)

This is an **optional rule** from the Dungeon Master's Guide. Check with your DM before assuming flanking is in play.

### How Flanking Works
When a creature and at least one of its allies are **on opposite sides or corners of an enemy**, they are flanking that enemy. Each flanking creature gains **advantage** on melee attack rolls against the flanked creature.

### Requirements
- **Grid-based combat required.** Flanking uses the optional grid rules.
- **Melee only.** Flanking advantage applies only to **melee attack rolls**, not ranged.
- **Opposite sides.** On a standard grid, two creatures flank an enemy when they are on **directly opposite sides** of the enemy's space. Draw an imaginary line between the centers of the two flankers' spaces -- if it passes through opposite sides or corners of the enemy's space, the enemy is flanked.
- **Must be able to act.** An incapacitated creature cannot flank. Both flanking creatures must be able to see and threaten the enemy.

### DM Considerations
Some DMs modify or don't use flanking because:
- It can make advantage too easy to obtain, reducing the value of other features that grant advantage.
- It encourages static melee positioning rather than dynamic combat.
- An alternative is granting **+2 to attack rolls** instead of advantage for flanking.

title: Climbing on Creatures
summary: Optional rule for climbing onto larger creatures using Athletics or Acrobatics checks.
content:
## Climbing on Creatures (Optional Rule)

This is an **optional rule** from the Dungeon Master's Guide. It provides a way for smaller creatures to fight larger foes in a more cinematic fashion.

### Requirements
- The target creature must be at least **two sizes larger** than you (e.g., a Medium creature can climb a Huge or larger creature).
- You must be in a position to physically reach and climb the creature.

### Making the Climb
As an **action** (or as part of your movement, at DM discretion), make a **Strength (Athletics)** or **Dexterity (Acrobatics)** check contested by the target's **Dexterity (Acrobatics)** check.

- **Success:** You move into the target creature's space and cling to its body. You move with the target and have **advantage on attack rolls** against it.
- **Failure:** You do not climb onto the creature. If you were already in its space, you are pushed to the nearest unoccupied space.

### Dislodging a Climber
The creature can use its **action** to try to dislodge you. It makes a **Strength (Athletics)** check contested by your **Strength (Athletics)** or **Dexterity (Acrobatics)** check. On success, you are thrown off and land prone in a space within 5 feet.

### Considerations
- **Precarious position.** The DM may rule that certain situations (the creature flying, diving underwater, rolling) automatically dislodge you or require additional checks.
- **Movement.** While climbing on a creature, your movement is limited to moving around on the creature's body.
- **Works like grappling.** Think of it as an alternative to grappling that's specifically for fighting giant enemies -- dragon riders, taking down an ogre, etc.
